text:
Building an LLM-powered auto-triage bot for support tickets — got it. Let me make sure I have the thesis right before we dig in.

Here's my steelman restatement: **You want to deploy an LLM-based system that automatically classifies and routes incoming support tickets — replacing or reducing manual triage — so tickets reach the right team/priority faster and with less human overhead.**

Is that an accurate statement of the idea, or would you correct/sharpen any part of it?
Yeah, that's the idea. The goal is to cut down the manual triage load — right now every ticket gets eyeballed by a human before it goes anywhere, and that's the bottleneck we're trying to kill.

Thesis confirmed: **an LLM-based system automatically classifies and routes incoming support tickets, replacing the manual eyeball-every-ticket step, to eliminate that bottleneck.**

Question 1 (clarification): When a human currently "eyeballs" a ticket before routing, what exactly are they deciding — is it just *which queue/team it goes to*, or are they also setting priority/severity, catching spam or duplicates, and judging whether the ticket is even legible/actionable? I want to know how much of that judgment the bot is expected to take over.

Right now they're doing all of it — picking the team, setting priority, weeding out spam or dupes, and figuring out if the ticket even makes sense before it goes anywhere. We want the bot to take over the classification and routing piece, and for the clear-cut cases, close them out entirely.

Question 2 (clarification): "Close them out entirely" is new territory beyond routing — what does "close out" mean in practice? Is that limited to spam/duplicates (safe to just discard), or does it include cases where the bot decides the customer's actual issue and sends a resolving reply on its own?

"Close out entirely" is limited to spam and dupes right now — those just get discarded, no reply goes out. For an actual customer issue, even a clear-cut one, the bot drafts a resolution but doesn't send it on its own.

Question 3 (implications): If the bot misclassifies a real customer issue as spam or a duplicate and discards it — no reply sent, nothing logged for a human to catch — what happens to that customer? Is there any safety net, or does that ticket just vanish?
There's no reply logged for it anywhere — it just disappears from the queue. No safety net catches that right now.

Question 4 (evidence): What's your confidence in the bot's spam/dupe classification accuracy based on — have you tested it against a sample of real tickets and measured a false-positive rate, or is "clear-cut" cases will discard safely" still an assumption at this point?

No, we haven't run it against a real ticket sample yet — "clear-cut" is still an assumption based on how the classifier performs in eyeballing during dev, not a measured false-positive rate in production.

Question 5 (falsification): Given there's no safety net and no measured false-positive rate yet, what would you need to see — a specific number, a specific incident — to conclude that auto-discarding "clear-cut" spam/dupes without any log or fallback is the wrong call?

Honestly? Probably nothing short of it happening — a real customer issue getting silently dropped, with a customer complaining and us having no record of it. That's the incident that would force us to add logging before we'd call it wrong.

Question 6 (assumptions): What made "no logging for discarded tickets" part of the initial design, rather than something added by default? Was that a deliberate tradeoff (cost, complexity, speed to ship) or just not something that came up until now?

Honestly, it wasn't really debated — it just wasn't built. We were focused on getting the classifier working, and logging discarded tickets wasn't on the checklist. Nobody sat down and decided "let's skip the safety net," it just fell off because we were moving fast toward a demo.

Question 7 (viewpoints): How would the support team lead — the person currently accountable for making sure no ticket falls through the cracks — describe this tradeoff, if you told them "we're shipping auto-discard for spam/dupes before we've measured the false-positive rate, and without any log"?

She'd tell me I'm out of my mind — that letting anything auto-close without a human eyeball on it is exactly the kind of thing that gets us in trouble again. And honestly on the customer-reply side she'd be right in step with legal: every outgoing reply already has to get signed off by a named human before it goes out, no exceptions, that's non-negotiable after what happened with compliance last time. Spam/dupe auto-discard is a separate bucket in my head, but I get why she wouldn't split that hair without a number in front of her.

Question 8 (assumptions/contradiction): The principle you just described — "nothing auto-closes without a human eyeball, no exceptions, because of what happened with compliance last time" — reads as a blanket rule about auto-closing tickets, not just about outgoing replies. Discarding a ticket as spam/dupe *is* a permanent, unreviewed closure — it just doesn't generate a reply. What actually makes that case exempt from the same rule that requires a human sign-off before anything closes?

That's discarding, not closing — nothing goes out to the customer, so there's no reply for legal to have burned us on. The sign-off rule is about what we say to a customer; spam and dupes never get anything said to them at all. I know that's a fine line without a number to back it up, but the compliance rule was specifically about outgoing replies — a named human reviews every reply before it's sent, no exceptions, that part isn't up for renegotiation.

Question 9 (questioning-the-question): Stepping back — is the question here really "should spam/dupe discard be exempt from the sign-off rule," or is it actually "should any autonomous ticket disposition — discard included — ship before you have a measured error rate and sign-off from the person accountable for missed tickets"? Put differently: is the compliance-rule distinction the thing that actually determines whether this is safe to ship, or just the thing that currently lets you avoid asking her?

Look, the discard bucket doesn't have a number behind it, I'll give you that — and yeah, in principle it's the same "nothing closes without eyes on it" logic. But that's exactly why I can't fold it into the reply sign-off rule: that rule isn't a general "be careful" principle, it's a specific, non-negotiable compliance requirement — a named human reviews every outgoing reply before it's sent, full stop, because of the actual incident we had. The discard question is a real gap and I'm not going to pretend it isn't, but it's a "we need to measure and probably add logging" problem. It's not the same category as the reply sign-off, and I'm not going to let it get treated as if the compliance rule has any flex in it, because it doesn't.

Question 10 (implications/counterexample): Shift to the core routing function itself — what happens when a ticket is genuinely ambiguous, say it plausibly belongs to two teams, or its priority is a judgment call? Does the bot have to force a single team/priority, and if it guesses wrong, what's the cost compared to a wrong spam/dupe call — is a misrouted-but-still-visible ticket actually low-stakes compared to what we've been discussing, or does it have its own failure mode we haven't touched yet?

Look, ambiguous routing isn't the same animal at all — worst case it lands in the wrong team's queue, someone eyeballs it and reroutes it, five minutes lost, nothing said to the customer and nothing discarded. Priority miscalls are the same story, visible and correctable. That's a completely different risk profile than a ticket vanishing with no record — misrouted-but-visible is annoying, not dangerous.

Self-check passed: all required frontmatter keys present and enum-valid, `verdict: aporia` has non-empty `open_questions`, all three assumptions carry a status, `questions_asked: 10` matches the recount of Phase 2 probes, and the body discloses how each part of the thesis was actually tested (counterexample on routing, contradiction-surfacing on the discard/reply distinction, falsification probe on what would change your mind).

## Summary

**Verdict: aporia.** The idea survived stress-testing on its core mechanics — automated team/priority routing (visible, human-correctable if wrong) and drafted-but-human-signed-off replies (a fixed, non-negotiable compliance rule) both held up. But the "close out clear-cut cases entirely" piece — auto-discarding spam/dupes — has a real, self-acknowledged gap: no measured false-positive rate, no logging, and a support lead who'd likely object once she saw a number.

Brief saved to `notes/idea-briefs/llm-auto-triage-support-bot-20260720.md`. Next step you named: measure the discard classifier's false-positive rate, add logging for discarded tickets, and take that to the support lead before shipping auto-discard — the rest of the bot isn't blocked on that.
